segments.text: Now, we come to the Sutan number 3, 06, 06, now Sangrava, the Brahmin came to see the exalted one.
As he said, at one side, he said this to the exalted one.
Master Gautama, we Brahmins, let me tell you, offer sacrifice and cause others to do so.
Therefore, Master Gautama, who so offers sacrifice or causes others to do so.
One and all are proficient in a practice of merit that affects many persons.
That is, which results from offering sacrifice.
Now, Master Gautama, he who goes forth as a wanderer from this or that family.
From the home to the homeless life, teams only the single self, comes only the single self, leads to Nirvana only the single self.
So, what I say is that he is proficient in a practice of merit that affects only one person as a result of his going forth.
Let me stop here to make a comment.
This, what the Brahmin is saying here is, is a kind of idea that a lot of people have that if a person practices this spiritual path, he is only helping himself.
In other words, he is more selfish than the person who does Dana makes offerings because making offerings, you help many people.
You are benefiting a lot of people, whereas you cultivate the holy life.
You become a monk or something, you are only helping yourself and that is selfish.
So, let's see what the Buddha said.
Well, Brahmin, as to that, I will question you.
Do you answer as you think of it?
Now, I think you, Brahmin.
In this connection, a tatagata arises in the world and Arahan, who is a fully enlightened one.
Perfect in knowledge and practice, welfare, will know incomparable chariot here of men to be tamed.
Teacher of Davas and mankind, a Buddha and exalted one.
He says, that, come, this is the way, this is the practice, proficient in which I make known,
that incomparable bliss which is steeped in the holy life by my own powers of comprehension, realizing it.
Come, you also practice so that you too may be proficient therein, so that you too, by your own powers of comprehension, may realize it and abide therein.
Thus, this teacher teaches Dhamma and others too practice to attain that end.
Moreover, there are many hundreds, many thousands, many hundreds of thousands of such.
Now, what I think you, Brahmin, since this is so, is it a practice of merit affecting only one person or many persons that is the result of going forth as a wanderer?
The Brahmin reply, no Master Gautama, it affects many persons this quick forth.
Let's stop here, for a while.
So here, the Buddha is saying, by becoming a Buddha, he has taught many hundreds of thousands of people to practice the holy path also.
And so the Brahmin had to admit, yes, that his going forth as a monk is helping a lot of people.
Now, just now, you were saying whether a monk, practicing the holy path, is selfish, is in our hands selfish.
Now, this selfishness, now, where does it come from?
Selfishness, meanness, doing wrong things, it all comes from the ego.
Because we have the ego, we have to protect the self.
And the self is not only this body of that person or that is associated with this body, that means like,
my husband, my wife, my children, my parents, my relatives, those of my own faith, those of my countrymen, those of my same village, etc., all this is associated with the self.
So, because of that, you see sometimes, race and race go to walk, kill each other, like in Africa, because the self, they don't try to protect the greater self.
That means all of those that belong to their race.
And sometimes, people of different religions also take the sword and kill each other, why?
Because they see that those of their own, of their same religion, part of their own self.
So, when we practice the spiritual path, we are trying to eliminate the ego.
We are trying to eliminate the self.
And when we eliminate the self and become an arahan, then there is no more self, then that person is the least selfish, because he has no more self.
So, he cannot be selfish at all.
That is why the Buddha said in Asuta, that if you wanted to take a knife and plunge it into an arahan's chest, he would never shout for help.
Because he has no more self, if you wanted to kill him, he would not resist.
So, you can see him, because an arahan has no self, he cannot be selfish at all.
That kind of person is the highest person in the world.
In fact, nobody can be as compassionate as the arahan.
The arahan sees that all beings are in his own self.
It is all in consciousness, all in his mind.
So, that is why you can never say that an arahan is selfish.
And even if a person doesn't become a Buddha or an arahan, by practicing the way, he is eliminating his ego to a certain extent.
That he is more harmless than an ordinary Buddha Janai, an ordinary person.
In fact, the ordinary person, the ordinary Buddha Janai, is the one that creates all the problems in the world.
If a lot of people became Arya, even the first stage Arya Sottapanna, then there would be very, very much less problems in the world.
So, if we help ourselves, we are helping others.
If we don't help ourselves, we cannot help others.
That's why in one Sutta, the Buddha said there are four kinds of persons.
The worst one is the one who does not help himself, does not help others.
Better than this person is the person who helps others, but does not help himself.
Then, even better than this person is the one who helps himself, but does not help others.
And the best is the one who helps himself and helps others.
So, you can see the second and the third person, the one who helps himself, is better than the one who helps others.
Because even if you help others, as long as you have the self, he will still love yourself the most.
Right? Nobody loves another as much as he himself is what the Buddha has said.
So, if we say that we want to help others, before helping ourselves, then we are being a bit hypocritical.
Because as long as we have the self, nobody is more important than ourselves.
Then, to continue this Sutta.
At these words, the verbal Ananda said this to the Brahmin Sangharava.
Of these two practices, Brahmin, which appears to you as being the simple, the last troublesome, of greater proof, of greater proof.
At these words, the Brahmin Sangharava said this to the verbal Ananda.
Just like the word he gotama and the word he Ananda, both are to me word he of honour and praise.
Then a second time, the verbal Ananda said to the Brahmin Sangharava,
I do not ask you that Brahmin as to whom you honour and praise.
This is what I am asking you, which of the two practices appears to you as being the simple, and less troublesome, of greater proof and proof.
Again, the Brahmin Sangharava said, just like the word he gotama and the word he Ananda, both are to me word he of honour and praise.
At the third time, the verbal Ananda put the same question to him.
At the third time, the Brahmin made the same reply.
I just stop here again to make another comment.
Here, the verbal Ananda is trying to ask him, which one do you think is simpler?
The one making the Ananda giving offerings to a lot of people or cultivating the holy life.
And the Brahmin Sangharava did not want to commit himself.
He didn't want to say which one is better because he felt that both are also good.
So he said, this is like the Venable, go to mind the Venable Ananda, both to me are word you honour and praise.
So he doesn't want to commit himself.
And then, there upon the exalted one thought, even for a third time, the Brahmin, on being asked a pertinent question by Ananda,
evades it, does not reply to it.
Suppose I release them from their difficulty.
So the exalted one said this to the Brahmin Sangharava.
Tell me, Brahmin, what was the topic of conversation today when the royal parties had together in company in the Raja's palace?
And he said, this was the topic of conversation master gotama.
In form of times, monks were fewer in number, but those possessed of supernormal powers were more numerous,
they showed the marvel of supernormal power.
But now it is just the opposite.
That was the topic of conversation today, master gotama, when the royal parties had together in company in the Raja's palace.
And the Buddha said, nice to that Brahmin, there are these three types of marvels, what three?
The marvel of supernormal power, the marvel of thought reading, and the marvel of teaching.
And what Brahmin is the marvel of supernormal power?
In this case, a certain one enjoys various sorts of supernormal power.
From being one he becomes many, from being many he becomes one.
When he first saw invisible, he goes and hindered through a war, through a rampart, through a mountain, as if through the air.
He plunges into the earth and shoots up again as if in water.
He walks upon the water without parting it, as if on solid ground.
He travels through the air, sitting cross-legged, like a bird upon the wing.
Even this moon and sun, though of such mighty power and majesty, he handles them and strokes them with his hand.
Even as far as the Brahma world he has power with his body, this Brahmin is called the marvel of supernormal power.
And what Brahmin is the marvel of thought reading?
In this case a certain one can declare by means of a sign.
Thus is your mind, such and such is your mind.
Thus is your consciousness, and however much he may tell, so it is and not otherwise.
And again Brahmin, perhaps a certain one does not declare such things by means of a sign,
but he does so after hearing a voice from men or not humans or from devours,
but he does so judging from some sound he has heard and utterance and intelligent
and utterance intelligently made by one who is reasoning in this place,
unintelligent and utterance intelligently made by one who is reasoning intelligently.
So, hearing he declares, thus is your mind, such and such is your mind, thus is your consciousness,
and however much he may tell, so it is and not otherwise.
Then, again Brahmin, in this case, suppose a certain one does not declare by any of these
ways yet may be, when he has attained a state of concentration which is void of thought,
light and sustained, once mind can be read, once mind can read another thoughts, and
knows thus, according to the nature of the thinking of this worthy, on such and such objects,
will he immediately, will he immediately direct his thoughts, then however much he may
tell, so it is and not otherwise, this Brahmin is called the marble of mind reading.
And what Brahmin is the marble of teaching, in this case a certain one teaches thus,
reason thus, not thus, apply your mind thus, not thus, abandon this state, acquire that state
and abide therein.
This Brahmin is called the marble of teaching, so these are the three marbles, now these
three marbles which appears to you as the more wonderful and excellent, and the Buddha
said.
And Brahmin said, or these marbles must go tamar, the marble of supernormal power, seems
to me to be of the nature of an illusion, then again as to the marble of thought reading,
he who performs it has the experience of the marble, it is a possession of him who performs
it, this also must go tamar seems to me to be of the nature of an illusion, but as to
the marble of teaching of these three marbles, this one appears to me as the more wonderful
and excellent, this stop here a while to commend this Brahmin, he shows some wisdom in saying
that the marble of teaching is more wonderful and excellent than the other two types of psychic
power which the Buddha described, and perhaps because he was familiar with certain different
ways of showing psychic power, we have to be very careful and sometimes some people nowadays
we see a very caught up with psychic power this place, because in the Buddha's time, even
from long ago, you we have heard that psychic power can be displayed by kind of black magic
ways, one is using mantras, mantras are not in the, there are other teachings, not in
the original teachings of the Buddha, and some of these mantras are very powerful and because
of that people like to use mantras, mantras are the words to have no meaning to us when
we recite the words, there is no meaning, and the meaning can only be understood by these spirits,
so the Buddha did say in some of the, in some sulta that he prepared his disciples to show
this psychic powers, because he said, if he showed psychic powers, if he allowed his disciples
to show psychic powers, those who have faith, then would have more faith, but those who did not
have faith, then would say that the Buddha's disciples are using black magic and using mantras,
and the Buddha quoted the names of certain mantras, which means that the Buddha was quite familiar
with his mantras, in one of the sultas, even some divas who used mantras, a very powerful
mantras, one of them, a nasu-raking, must be born in hell for using his mantras, so that is one
way that people sometimes exhibit psychic power, mantras, and another one is keeping spirits
using spirits to work for them, and then another one could be using the help of some divas,
but sometimes they use it to put good purpose to heal people, and all that now,
it could also be used for bad purpose, so the point is to be very careful when people try to
heal that they have psychic power, or they show that they have psychic power, because a truly
virtuous person would not show even if that person has psychic power, he would not show of it,
because it is a sign, generally, of ego, to show of these psychic powers, and then to continue
this with term, Brahmin said, it is strange must go tamar, it is wonderful how well the
worthy go tamar has spoken of this thing, be to hold the worthy go tamar to possess all three
miles, surely the worthy go tamar enjoys various kinds of supernatural, from being one he becomes
many etc, etc. surely the worthy go tamar's mind can read another thoughts, many as a day,
in the state of concentration, void of thought, applied and sustained, as to as to know,
according to the nature of the thinking of this worthy, one of such and such objects will
he immediately direct his thoughts, again surely the worthy go tamar teaches, reason does and thus
apply your mind thus, not thus, abandon this state, acquire that state and apply in the
harin, then the Buddha said, indeed Brahmin, your words come close and challenge me to a statement,
nevertheless I will satisfy you by replying, I do indeed possess these supernatural powers Brahmin,
I do read thoughts, I do so instruct, and then the Brahmin said, but master go tamar is there any
other single man possessed of these three marvelous powers besides the worthy go tamar,
in the Buddha replied, yes indeed Brahmin, the monks possessed of these three marvelous powers
are not just one or two or three, four or five hundred, but much more than that in number,
and in the Brahmin asked, pre master go tamar, where are those monks now dwelling and the Buddha said,
in this very order of monks Brahmin, the Brahmin said, excellent master go tamar,
excellent it is master go tamar, even as one raises up what is overtoned or holds up a light
in the darkness, even in diverse ways has tamar been set forth by the worthy go tamar,
I myself to go for refuge to the worthy go tamar to tamar and to the order of monks,
me the worthy go tamar except me as a lay follower who has taken refuge in him,
that's the end of the Buddha. Now this last part now, you find quite interesting is that
in the Rogers palace they were discussing that formally, a lot of monks, a lot of renunciants
had psyche power, but nowadays very few renunciants had psyche power, and then what the Buddha
is saying is actually there are a lot more than 500, which shows that the people during the Buddha's
time they were not aware that all these monks with psyche power were around, which brings the
point I made just now that virtuous monks never display the attainment. The Buddha said that
told the monks that even if they have attainance that they should not speak of it,
that should not hint of it, and if they don't have attainment and they say that they have
attainment, then it's a very serious offense, highest, the most serious offense they
are poor, offenses, which if a monk that indulges in practices, then he is no more a monk,
even though he wears the rope, the first one he engages in sexual intercourse, the second one,
he steals something which is valuable, and according to some interpretation what is valuable
means something that is worth more than 50 ringgit or 20 US dollars, and third one is he kills a human
being or he encourages the killing of another human being, even asking a human to take a
contraceptive to a boy, a child is also included, and the both one if he does not have
supernatural attainment, and he sees that he has these four ways called prasika offenses whereby
even monk breaks them, he is no more a monk, even though he wears the rope, and he's not allowed
to ordain again in this very lifetime, so in that respect, a monk is not even supposed to
praise his teacher, and I say that his teacher is such an area or something, it's not allowed,
and then another interesting thing about this utah, as you see, this man has challenged
the Buddha to make a reply, he's saying that I think you have these psychic powers, and
he's saying, do you actually have these psychic powers, and the Buddha, since he was challenging,
the Buddha did confirm that he has these psychic powers, but generally it's the Buddha that
can make this kind of statement, because the Buddha is supposed to have said that these
binayah rules that he made for his disciples, they're literally not points, but I'm not sure whether
if a monk is actually confronted with such a question, whether he can actually say that he has
this psychic powers, I think if he's about to smong probably, he will not, so even he can,
and the other thing is he hears that the Buddha is also saying that because he was asked,
where are these monks with psychic powers now reciting, so he's saying that he's been
is to be found in his order of monks, the last thing I have to point out here is you see,
in this is one of the zuta where he goes, that lay people when they take refuge with the triple
gem, he is taking refuge with the Buddha, damma, which is the Buddha's teachings, and the third one
is the Bikku Sangha, he takes refuge with the order of monks, community of monks, Bikku Sangha,
that's the Pali word, so, of course sometimes it is explained elsewhere that the Bikku Sangha
is also the bow, the eight types of Aryan, the bow pairs of Aryas, but it's the Pali word
to note, as always, the Bikku Sangha, that he takes refuge.
